stories.name: Barny Dillarstone
stories.story_summary: British marine conservationist, scuba diver, and YouTube filmmaker who pioneered a DIY 'deep sea camera drop' methodology using a weighted cage rig with an Insta360 Ace Pro 2, lowered 200 metres into the Bali Sea off Nusa Penida (Black Magic Island), Indonesia. In February 2026 his rig captured what marine experts believe is the first-ever video of a purple eagle ray alive in the wild, as well as the first-ever filmed individual western highfin spurdog (Squalus albifascia). His ongoing Shark Cam Series has been producing citizen science discoveries since at least 2024, with expeditions in Amed, Bali (November 2024) and Nusa Penida (February 2026). Covered by Digital Camera World, PetaPixel, LadBible, TechEBlog, GreaterGood, and IndoDefenceReview.
stories.why_it_matters: World-first scientific footage — a purple eagle ray and western highfin spurdog never before filmed alive — achieved with a $400 Insta360 action camera in a homemade rig. The most powerful argument for camera democratisation: one affordable action camera producing discoveries that professional expeditions with million-dollar budgets haven't made. Citizen science + conservation + filmmaking innovation in one story.
stories.outreach_draft:
Subject: Insta360 wants to tell the Barny Dillarstone story

Hi Barny,

I'm [Name] from Insta360's creator partnerships team. We've been following your Shark Cam Series closely — and your February 2026 expedition off Nusa Penida stopped us in our tracks.

A world-first video of a purple eagle ray alive in the wild, filmed with a homemade rig and an Insta360 Ace Pro 2 lowered 200 metres into the Bali Sea. Digital Camera World, PetaPixel, and LadBible covered it — but we think the full story deserves more.

We'd love to feature you as an Insta360 Creator Story: the DIY engineering behind the cage rig, the citizen science mission, and what it means to film species that million-dollar expeditions have missed. We'd also love to explore how we can support future dives — whether through equipment, amplifying your discoveries, or connecting you with our conservation community.

Would you be open to a conversation?

Warm regards,
[Name]
Insta360 Creator Partnerships

stories.name: Jenny Stock
stories.story_summary: Award-winning underwater cinematographer and BBC documentary producer named British Underwater Photographer of the Year 2024. Stock used the Insta360 X4 to win 3rd place in the Compact Camera category of the prestigious Underwater Photographer of the Year competition, and has integrated it into her marine conservation filming work alongside a 24-year broadcast TV career including National Geographic and BBC. A speaker at Go Diving Show 2025 and author of underwater photography books.
stories.why_it_matters: A credentialed broadcast filmmaker with National Geographic and BBC credits who has publicly championed Insta360 X4 as a competition-grade tool for underwater conservation filmmaking — winning an industry award with it. Her endorsement would resonate powerfully with professional filmmakers on the fence about adopting Insta360.
stories.outreach_draft:
Subject: Your Insta360 X4 competition win caught our eye

Insta360 Creator Partnerships

stories.name: Ben Forsyth
stories.story_summary: Australian documentary filmmaker (Think Films) directing 'Ultra,' a feature documentary about undefeated plant-based ultra-endurance cyclist Abdullah Zeinab preparing for the 4000 km Transcontinental Race across Europe. Forsyth uses Insta360 GO 3 cameras in a body-worn configuration to capture intimate footage of Zeinab's psychological inner experience during extreme endurance — a creative approach Think Films describes as 'a first in the global documentary film world.' Zeinab has won every race he has entered, including the 2019 6,800 km Trans-America Bike Race.
stories.why_it_matters: An organic documentary project using Insta360's smallest camera to solve a real storytelling challenge: how to document the internal psychological landscape of an athlete during a 4000 km race. Think Films' body-worn Insta360 GO 3 approach is a genuine creative breakthrough in sports documentary filmmaking.
stories.outreach_draft:

stories.name: Peter Mieras
stories.location: Port Alberni, Vancouver Island, British Columbia, Canada
stories.story_summary: Underwater filmmaker and dive operator Peter Mieras lowered his Insta360 X4 on a fishing line off his dock in Barkley Sound. When the line snapped, the camera sank 30 feet and captured viral footage of a humpback whale charging through a bait ball of herring. Footage covered by PetaPixel, TechEBlog, My Modern Met, The Inertia, and multiple BC local newspapers in January 2025.
stories.why_it_matters: Accidental viral footage captured with an Insta360 X4 demonstrates the camera's durability and underwater capability in a compelling origin story. Mieras is a credible filmmaker and dive operator with decades of ocean experience, making this an authentic use-case story with broad media coverage and a shareable moment.
stories.verification_note: Product name 'Insta360 X4' explicitly stated in multiple independent sources: TechEBlog headline reads 'Videographer Puts Insta360 X4 Camera on Fishing Line, Stunning Whale Footage Ensues'; Kelowna Capital News states 'He has been filming from his dock with an Insta360 X4 camera'. Covered by at least 6 independent outlets.

stories.name: John Kowitz
stories.story_summary: Underwater photographer John Kowitz spotted a humpback whale entangled in over 320 feet of fishing gear off Maui and used his Insta360 camera underwater to document the full extent of the entanglement. His footage provided crucial visual data that helped NOAA's entanglement response team plan a successful rescue. The team removed 251 feet of line, a 10-foot chain, and several hooks over a 5-hour operation. Covered by PetaPixel, My Modern Met, Upworthy, and Green Matters in March 2026.
stories.why_it_matters: Real-world conservation impact: Insta360 footage directly enabled a successful whale rescue operation. Kowitz is a respected underwater photographer with 72K Instagram followers and a track record in marine conservation storytelling. The story has strong emotional resonance and demonstrates Insta360 as a tool for meaningful action.
stories.verification_note: Multiple independent search result summaries from PetaPixel, My Modern Met, and Green Matters reference Kowitz using an Insta360 camera. Direct article access blocked by 403 errors. Specific model not confirmed. Human reviewer should verify exact quote from PetaPixel article before outreach.
stories.outreach_draft:

stories.name: Xiaolin Sun
stories.location: Ningbo, China (University of Nottingham Ningbo China)
stories.story_summary: PhD candidate Xiaolin Sun traveled to Miao indigenous villages in Guizhou Province, China, using an Insta360 X3 to capture over 500GB of 360-degree video of village life, traditional dance, and craftsmanship. The footage formed the basis of an immersive museum experience. Sun co-authored a peer-reviewed study published in Humanities and Social Sciences Communications (Nature portfolio journal, July 2025, Article 1014) demonstrating that 360-degree documentary significantly increases viewer enjoyment, cultural presence, and curiosity compared to traditional 2D documentary.
stories.why_it_matters: First peer-reviewed study in a Nature portfolio journal to explicitly name Insta360 in a cultural preservation methodology. Proves with academic rigor that Insta360's 360-degree format creates measurably superior cultural engagement — a powerful third-party validation for Insta360's positioning in education and cultural heritage sectors.
stories.verification_note: PMC/Google Scholar snippet explicitly states 'An Insta360 X3 camera captured over 500 GB of 360-degree video footage' in the methods section. Paper confirmed published in Humanities and Social Sciences Communications (Nature portfolio, ISSN 2662-9992). Direct full article access blocked (403) on nature.com. Recommend human reviewer verify via institutional journal access before outreach.
stories.outreach_draft:
